plainMeaning: Living a good, righteous life is the greatest blessing of all — and someone whose mind is always fixed on it is so admirable that even the gods bow to them.
recitation: dhammo maṅgalam-ukkiṭhaṁ, ahiṁsā saṁjamo tavo; devā vi taṁ namaṁsanti, jassa dhamme sayā maṇo.

wordByWord.gloss: dharma — righteous religion / the moral law (here, the subject of the sentence)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhamma < Sanskrit dharma, from √dhṛ 'to hold, uphold'; nominative singular dhammo.
wordByWord.notes: Defined in this very verse by its three components in the next words.

wordByWord.gloss: the highest auspiciousness — sandhi compound of maṅgalam ('auspiciousness, blessing') + ukkiṭṭhaṁ ('topmost, supreme')
wordByWord.etymology: maṅgala (auspiciousness) + Prakrit ukkiṭṭha < Sanskrit utkṛṣṭa ('drawn up, exalted, supreme', ut- + √kṛṣ); neuter nominative agreeing with dhammo.
wordByWord.notes: The book reads ઠ (-kiṭha-); the widely attested form is ઉક્કિટ્ઠ -ukkiṭṭha-. Verbatim spelling preserved.

wordByWord.gloss: non-violence — the first constituent of dharma
wordByWord.notes: Stands in apposition, unpacking what dhamma is.

wordByWord.gloss: self-restraint, control of senses and conduct — the second constituent of dharma
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit saṁjama < Sanskrit saṁyama (sam- + √yam 'to restrain'); nominative singular saṁjamo.

wordByWord.gloss: austerity, ascetic discipline (tapas) — the third constituent of dharma
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tava < Sanskrit tapas, from √tap 'to heat, to be austere'; nominative tavo.
wordByWord.notes: ahiṁsā + saṁyama + tapas together form the practical substance of dharma.

wordByWord.gloss: even the gods — devā ('gods', nom. pl.) + vi ('even, also')
wordByWord.etymology: devā < deva (from √div 'to shine'); vi is Prakrit for Sanskrit api ('even, also'), here emphatic.
wordByWord.notes: The emphatic 'even' is the rhetorical pivot of the second line: not merely humans, but celestials, do the bowing.

wordByWord.gloss: him / that one — the person described in the relative clause that follows
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit taṁ < Sanskrit tam, accusative singular of the demonstrative tad.
wordByWord.notes: Antecedent of jassa ('whose'); the object of veneration is the practitioner, not an abstraction.

wordByWord.gloss: they bow to / venerate / pay homage
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit namaṁsanti < Sanskrit namasyanti, denominative from namas ('homage'), √nam 'to bend'; 3rd person plural present.
wordByWord.notes: Subject is devā; object is taṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jassa < Sanskrit yasya, genitive singular of the relative pronoun yad.
wordByWord.notes: Opens the relative clause defining who the gods bow to.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhamme < Sanskrit dharme, locative singular of dharma.
wordByWord.notes: Locative of the object on which the mind rests.
Stresses constancy: not occasional piety but unbroken orientation.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit maṇa < Sanskrit manas ('mind'); nominative maṇo, with the copula 'is' understood.
wordByWord.notes: Subject of the relative clause: '[he] whose mind is ever in dharma'.

literalTranslation: Dharma is the supreme auspiciousness — [namely] non-violence, self-restraint, and austerity. Even the gods bow to him whose mind is ever [set] on dharma.
idiomaticTranslation: Righteousness is the highest blessing of all — and its very substance is non-harming, self-mastery, and disciplined austerity. The one whose mind rests forever in such righteousness is revered even by the gods, who bow before him.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This opening gāthā is the doctrinal seed from which the whole chapter grows; everything that follows is illustration. Two moves are made. First, dharma is not merely praised but defined: maṅgalam-ukkiṭṭhaṁ asserts its rank — the *highest* auspiciousness, above ritual omens, wealth, or worldly good fortune — and then ahiṁsā saṁjamo tavo gives its content. The verse refuses to leave 'dharma' abstract: it is exactly non-harming, restraint, and austerity, the lived conduct (cāritra) of a śramaṇa. The triad is also a graded path — ahiṁsā guards others, saṁyama guards the senses inward, tava burns off accumulated karma — so the line moves from outward to inward to transformative. Second, the verse measures dharma's worth not by argument but by witness: devā vi taṁ namaṁsanti. The force lies in 'vi' (even). Gods, by Jain reckoning, far outrank humans in lifespan, radiance, and merit-enjoyment — yet they cannot practice this dharma, bound as they are to celestial pleasure. So they bow. Crucially the object is taṁ, *him* — the antecedent of jassa — not dharma in the abstract. What the gods venerate is a person: 'one whose mind is ever in dharma' (jassa dhamme sayā maṇo). The locus of holiness is thus relocated from heaven to the disciplined human heart, and the qualifier sayā ('always') sets the bar — steady, unbroken orientation of mind, not sporadic observance. The verse therefore both exalts dharma and quietly inverts the usual hierarchy: the embodied, restrained practitioner stands higher than the gods who salute him.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The triad ahiṁsā–saṁyama–tava is the conduct-aspect (cāritra-dharma) of the Jain path; ahiṁsā is the cardinal vow underlying all five mahāvratas, saṁyama the guarding of the seventeen kinds of restraint, and tapas the means of saṁvara/nirjarā (stopping and shedding karma). The closing claim — gods venerate the dharma-fixed person — reflects the Jain teaching that the deva-gati, though pleasurable, lacks the right circumstances (saṁyama) for liberation; only the human, through restraint and austerity, can advance toward mokṣa. Hence the constancy of mind (sayā maṇo) marks the soul oriented toward release, which even celestials honour.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter, this verse reframes 'auspiciousness': good fortune is not sought in omens or external blessing but cultivated as harmlessness, restraint, and self-discipline. The test it sets is internal and continuous — sayā, 'always': is the mind habitually turned toward non-harm and restraint, or only during recitation? Keeping it as the first sajjhāy verse fixes that question at the threshold of study.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.6 (tapasā nirjarā ca) and the role of saṁvara/nirjarā
Following verse, Daśavaikālika 1.2 (the bee-and-blossom simile that illustrates this dharma in conduct)

sources.title: Daśavaikālika (Daśavaikālikasūtra) — definition, authorship and canonical status
sources.consultedFor: Mūlasūtra status, attribution to the monk Śayyambhava (Sijjaṃbhava), and the ten-chapter structure

sources.title: Dharma — Jain and general definitions
sources.consultedFor: Semantic range of dharma (righteousness, moral law) underlying the verse's key term

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Dictionaries)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology of utkṛṣṭa (ut- + √kṛṣ, 'supreme'), maṅgala, tapas, saṁyama, and namas for the per-token glosses

notes: This is among the most widely attested verses of the Śvetāmbara canon; reading and meaning are secure. Orthography follows the Anchalgachchha book verbatim: મુક્કિઠં (-ukkiṭha-) here lacks the doubled conjunct of the more common ઉક્કિટ્ઠં (-ukkiṭṭhaṁ < utkṛṣṭa); the standard chanting form is given in 'recitation' with the variant flagged. The accusative તં (taṁ) is taken, with the standard commentaries, to refer to the person ('him whose mind is ever in dharma'), the antecedent of જસ્સ (jassa); some renderings loosely make 'dharma' itself the object of the gods' homage — the relative clause favours the personal reading. Prakrit ↔ Sanskrit correspondences (dhammo=dharmaḥ, saṁjamo=saṁyamaḥ, tavo=tapaḥ, devā vi=devā api, sayā=sadā, maṇo=manaḥ) are regular and uncontested.
